course_info.title: International Studies Capstone
prerequisites: IS411 - Research Methods in International Studies

class_schedule.topic: Capstone Overview: From Research Question to Policy-Relevant Output
learning_objectives: Understand capstone requirements, timeline, and assessment criteria
Refine a research question suitable for a semester-long capstone project
Develop a project plan with milestones and deliverables
readings: Selected exemplary capstone projects from prior years

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone is your opportunity to demonstrate mastery of international studies by producing an original, policy-relevant research project. This is the culmination of your degree.
key_concepts: Capstone project types: research thesis, policy analysis, applied project
From IS411 proposal to capstone: refining scope and feasibility
Project management: milestones, drafts, feedback cycles
Faculty advisor relationship and expectations
Professional standards: this should be portfolio-quality work
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: peer review of initial research proposals to refine scope and feasibility
setup_requirements: Draft research proposals from each student

review.value: Research thesis (original scholarly contribution), policy analysis (professional policy recommendation), or applied project (practical output like a program design, database, or simulation)
The capstone should demonstrate skills employable in government, NGOs, international organizations, or graduate study -- it is your professional calling card

learning_objectives: Draft and refine an argumentative literature review
Identify the gap your research will fill
Position your contribution within existing scholarship
readings: Selected literature reviews from published IS articles

lecture_notes.introduction: A literature review is not a bibliography. It is an argument about what we know, what we don't know, and why your research matters.
key_concepts: Argumentative structure: organizing by theme, debate, or chronology
Identifying the gap: what has not been studied, tested, or addressed
Synthesizing rather than summarizing
Critical evaluation of existing methods and findings
Transitioning from review to your contribution
lecture_notes.demonstration: Peer workshop: exchanging and critiquing draft literature reviews

review.value: A review that does not merely describe prior research but makes an argument about the state of knowledge, identifies tensions and gaps, and positions your research as filling a specific need
Grouping sources by theme or argument rather than reviewing them one by one, showing how different studies relate to each other and to your research question

class_schedule.topic: Methodology Workshop: Designing Your Capstone Research
learning_objectives: Defend your case selection or data collection strategy
readings: Methodology chapters from two published IS theses

lecture_notes.introduction: Your methodology must be rigorous enough to produce credible findings and feasible enough to complete in one semester. Today we pressure-test your research designs.
key_concepts: Matching methods to questions: qualitative, quantitative, mixed
Feasibility assessment: data access, time constraints, ethical approval
Operationalizing variables: turning concepts into measurable indicators
Pilot testing: testing your methods before full data collection
Handling obstacles: what is your Plan B?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hot seat methodology defense: each student presents their methodology for group critique

review.value: The process of defining how abstract concepts (democracy, development, security) will be measured in your specific research -- a critical step that determines what your data can actually tell you
Honest evaluation of whether your proposed research can be completed given available data, time, resources, language skills, and access -- the most common reason capstones fail is overambition

class_schedule.topic: Data Collection and Preliminary Analysis Workshop
learning_objectives: Report on data collection progress and challenges
Present preliminary findings for peer feedback
Adjust methodology based on early findings
readings: Selected methodology readings relevant to individual projects

lecture_notes.introduction: Data collection rarely goes as planned. Today we troubleshoot, share early findings, and help each other navigate the messy reality of research.
key_concepts: Common data collection challenges and solutions
Preliminary analysis: looking for patterns before full analysis
Iteration: adjusting your approach based on what you find
Data management and organization best practices
Maintaining a research journal
lecture_notes.demonstration: Progress reports: each student presents data collection status and preliminary findings

review.value: The reality that research is not linear: early findings may require revisiting your question, adjusting your methodology, or collecting additional data -- flexibility within rigor
Organizing, labeling, backing up, and documenting your data systematically so that your analysis is reproducible and your findings can be verified

learning_objectives: Present full analysis for peer review
Identify patterns, anomalies, and alternative explanations
Develop the argument that links evidence to conclusions
readings: Analysis chapters from published IS theses

lecture_notes.introduction: Analysis is where data becomes knowledge. The challenge is to let the evidence speak while maintaining the discipline of your research design.
key_concepts: Moving from description to analysis to argument
Pattern identification and significance assessment
Alternative explanations: ruling them out or acknowledging them
Connecting findings back to theory and literature
Honest reporting: what your data can and cannot support
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis workshop: small groups review and critique each other's analytical arguments

review.value: Competing explanations for your findings that must be explicitly addressed: either ruled out through evidence or acknowledged as limitations of your research
Reporting what you actually found, not what you hoped to find -- including null results, unexpected findings, and limitations -- which paradoxically strengthens rather than weakens your credibility

learning_objectives: Present completed capstone research to faculty and peers
Defend methodology, findings, and conclusions under questioning

lecture_notes.introduction: The capstone defense is the culmination of your undergraduate international studies education. Present your best work and defend it with confidence and intellectual honesty.
key_concepts: Effective research presentation: structure, timing, visual aids
Anticipating and addressing questions and critiques
Framing policy implications of academic research
Revision strategy: incorporating defense feedback into final draft
Professional development: how to use your capstone going forward
lecture_notes.demonstration: Formal capstone presentations and faculty/peer questioning

review.value: A formal presentation followed by questioning from faculty and peers, evaluating the rigor, originality, and significance of your research -- and your ability to defend your choices
Incorporating feedback from the defense into a final revised draft, demonstrating the ability to respond constructively to scholarly critique

major_assignments.title: Capstone Research Project
major_assignments.description: Produce an original, policy-relevant research project demonstrating mastery of international studies knowledge and research methods. The project must include a research question, literature review, methodology, original analysis, and conclusions with policy implications.
objectives: Conduct original research on an international studies topic
Demonstrate mastery of appropriate research methods
Defend research choices and findings under questioning
deliverables: 10000-15000 word capstone thesis or policy analysis

criteria: Clear identification of gap and contribution
Clear argument linking evidence to conclusions
Original and significant contribution to international studies knowledge
Clear argument with honest acknowledgment of limitations

capstone_project.description: The capstone thesis or policy analysis, including all components and milestones

capstone_defense.description: Formal presentation and oral defense of the capstone project

workshop_participation.description: Active engagement in peer review workshops and feedback sessions

milestone_completion.description: Timely completion of project milestones: proposal, literature review, methodology, draft, final

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all workshop sessions and all capstone presentations. Missing a workshop without prior approval results in a 5% grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Milestone deadlines are firm. Late milestones are penalized 10% per day because they affect peer review schedules.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review is collaborative and expected. The capstone project itself must be entirely individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: The capstone must be original work. Plagiarism or data fabrication results in course failure and degree delay. AI tools may assist with research but may not generate the submitted text.
course_policies.technology_requirements: All tools needed for your specific methodology: statistical software, qualitative analysis tools, reference management, and presentation software.
